message.content:
The fog on Blackwood Rock didn’t just obscure vision; it swallowed sound. For Elias Thorne, the lighthouse keeper, the silence was a companion he had learned to respect. For forty years, he had climbed the three hundred steps of the spiral stair, polished the Fresnel lens, and watched the Atlantic chew at the granite base of his home. He was a man of routine, of tides and timetables, of solitude so profound it had become a second skin.

The bottle arrived on a Tuesday, wedged into a crevice of the rocks near the tide pool where Elias collected his fresh water. It wasn’t the usual debris—no rotting wood, no plastic wrappers. It was a thick, green glass cylinder, sealed with wax that smelled faintly of lavender and old paper.

Elias picked it up, his calloused fingers brushing the condensation. He expected a plea for help, or perhaps a drunken sailor’s confession. He pried the cork out with his knife and pulled out a single sheet of heavy, cream-colored stationery.

The handwriting was elegant, looping, and unmistakably familiar.

*Dearest Elias,*

*If you are reading this, I have finally stopped running. I know you hate me for leaving. I know you think I chose the city, the noise, the people, over the quiet life we built here. But I didn’t leave because I wanted to escape you. I left because I was terrified that if I stayed, I would never truly see you. I needed to find myself so I could return to us as a whole person, not just a shadow in your light.*

*I am coming back. Not to ask for forgiveness, but to ask for a chance to start over. Meet me at the base of the tower at high tide, three days from now. If you aren’t there, I will assume
